4ae7443df944fa550c11213c4137f4bec29abbdf.text:
Based on the provided context, it appears that the documentation does not cover deploying an application using Azure Container Instances (ACI). The context only discusses Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) and Azure Functions with Container Apps.

To deploy applications using ACI, you can refer to the official Microsoft documentation:

[Source: azure/Azure Container Instances/Overview](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/overview)

Here's a brief overview of how to create and run a container instance using the Azure CLI:

1. Install the Azure CLI if you haven't already: [Source: azure/Azure CLI/Install Azure CLI](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/install-azure-cli)

2. Sign in to your Azure account:
   ```
   az login
   ```

3. Create a resource group:
   ```
   az group create --name myResourceGroup --location eastus
   ```

4. Create an Azure Container Instance:
   ```
   az container create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myContainerInstance --image mcr.microsoft.com/azuredocs/aci-helloworld --ports 80
   ```

5. To get the logs and status of your container instance:
   ```
   az container logs myContainerInstance
   ```

6. To delete the container instance:
   ```
   az container delete --name myContainerInstance --resource-group myResourceGroup
   ```

077357fc6611b8d2b96b79136c0c5661e37f7d62.text:
To deploy an application using Azure Blob Storage, you can follow these steps as described in the provided context for the Azure Blob Storage client library for Python:

1. Install the required packages: In the local directory, install packages for the Azure Blob Storage and Azure Identity client libraries using the following command: `pip install azure-storage-blob azure-identity` [Source: 1]

2. Update the storage account name: In the local directory, edit the file named **blob_quickstart.py**. Find the `<storage-account-name>` placeholder and replace it with the actual name of the storage account created by the `azd up` command. Save the changes. [Source: 1]

3. Initialize the Azure Developer CLI template and deploy resources: From an empty directory, follow these steps to initialize the `azd` template, provision Azure resources, and get started with the code:
   - Clone the quickstart repository assets from GitHub and initialize the template locally:

     ```
     git clone https://github.com/Azure-Samples/storage-quickstarts-python.git
     cd storage-quickstarts-python
     azd init
     ```
   - Log in to Azure:

     ```
     az login
     ```
   - Provision and deploy the resources to Azure:

     ```
     azd up
     ```

   You'll be prompted for the following information:
   - **Environment name**: This value is used as a prefix for all Azure resources created by Azure Developer CLI. The name must be unique across all Azure subscriptions and must be between 3 and 24 characters long. The name can contain numbers and lowercase letters only.
   - **Subscription**: The Azure subscription that your resources are deployed to.
   - **Location**: The Azure region where your resources are deployed. [Source: 4]

4. Run the project: Execute the following command to run the app: `python blob_quickstart.py`. [Source: 1]

5. Observe the output: This app creates a test file in your local *data* folder and uploads it to a container in the storage account. The example then lists the blobs in the container and downloads the file with a new name so that you can compare the old and new files. [Source: 1]

When you're finished testing the code, see the [Clean up resources](#clean-up-resources) section to delete the resources created by the `azd up` command. [Source: 1]

5beca4c256eba3f84bc08173524113e791dfbaa5.text:
To configure networking for Azure Blob Storage, follow these steps as outlined in the provided context:

1. Create an Azure virtual network [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Mount Blob Storage by using the Network File System (NFS) 3.0 protocol > Step 1: Create an Azure virtual network]
   - Learn more about Azure Virtual Network and how to create a virtual network in the [Virtual Network documentation](../../virtual-network/index.yml).

2. Mount Blob Storage by using the Network File System (NFS) 3.0 protocol [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Mount Blob Storage by using the Network File System (NFS) 3.0 protocol > Step 3: Create and configure a storage account]
   - To mount a container by using NFS 3.0, you must create a storage account. You can't enable existing accounts.
   - Choose these values for the storage account configuration:
     |Setting | Premium performance | Standard performance
     |----|---|---|
     |Location|All available regions |All available regions
     |Performance|Premium| Standard
     |Account kind|BlockBlobStorage| General-purpose V2
     |Replication|Locally-redundant storage (LRS), Zone-redundant storage (ZRS)| Locally-redundant storage (LRS), Zone-redundant storage (ZRS)
     |Connectivity method|Public endpoint (selected networks) or Private endpoint |Public endpoint (selected networks) or Private endpoint
     |Hierarchical namespace|Enabled|Enabled
     |NFS V3|Enabled |Enabled
   - Accept the default values for all other settings.

3. Configure the virtual network and subnet [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Troubleshoot issues in BlobFuse > Common mount problems > Failed to initialize new pipeline [configuration error in azstorage [account name not provided]]]
   - Select `dfs`.
   - Select Virtual network and Subnet.
   - Select DNS.
   - Select Yes for Integrate with private DNS.
   - Select the Subscription and Resource Group for your private link DNS.
   - Select Next, Next, and select Create.

4. (Optional) If you want to mount a container from a client that runs in an on-premises network, connect your on-premises network to your virtual network [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Mount Blob Storage by using the Network File System (NFS) 3.0 protocol > Step 1: Create an Azure virtual network]
   - See [Supported network connections](network-file-system-protocol-support.md#supported-network-connections).

bf9e055198ab970bf3dad17c79a2b772e8143948.text:
To configure networking for Azure Cosmos DB, follow these steps as described in the provided context:

1. [Configure a service endpoint by using the Azure portal > Configure a service endpoint for an existing Azure virtual network and subnet] (Source: azure/Cosmos DB/Configure access to Azure Cosmos DB from virtual networks > Configure a service endpoint by using the Azure portal > Configure a service endpoint for an existing Azure virtual network and subnet)
   - Navigate to the Azure Cosmos DB account that you want to secure.
   - Select Networking from the settings menu.
   - Choose to allow access from Selected networks.
   - To grant access to an existing virtual network's subnet, under Virtual networks, select Add existing Azure virtual network.
   - Select the Subscription from which you want to add an Azure virtual network.
   - Select the Azure Virtual networks and Subnet(s) that you want to provide access to your Azure Cosmos DB account.
   - Next, select Enable to enable selected networks with service endpoints for Microsoft.AzureCosmosDB. When it's complete, select Add.

2. [Configure Azure Private Link for an Azure Cosmos DB account > Create a private endpoint by using the Azure portal] (Source: azure/Cosmos DB/Configure Azure Private Link for an Azure Cosmos DB account > Create a private endpoint by using the Azure portal)
   - Sign in to the Azure portal, then select an Azure Cosmos DB account.
   - Select Networking from the list of settings, and then select + Private endpoint under the Private access tab.
   - Follow the steps provided in the documentation to create a private endpoint for your Azure Cosmos DB account using the Azure portal.

3. [Configure access to Azure Cosmos DB from virtual networks > Configure a service endpoint by using the Azure portal > Configure a service endpoint for a new Azure virtual network and subnet] (Source: azure/Cosmos DB/Configure access to Azure Cosmos DB from virtual networks > Configure a service endpoint by using the Azure portal > Configure a service endpoint for a new Azure virtual network and subnet)
   - If you want to create a new Azure virtual network, follow the steps provided in the documentation to do so and enable service endpoint for Microsoft.AzureCosmosDB.

4. [Configure IP firewall for Azure Cosmos DB > IP access control] (Source: azure/Cosmos DB/Configure IP firewall for Azure Cosmos DB > IP access control)
   - To configure IP-based firewall rules, you can set up a list of allowed client IPs in CIDR form to restrict access to your Azure Cosmos DB account. After applying this configuration, any requests from machines outside the allowed list receive a 403 (Forbidden) response.

35a0c0df89ac1eac32de3cda5fcd8725293a93ad.text:
To set up monitoring and alerts for Azure Monitor, follow these steps:

1. Navigate to your Azure Virtual Machine or Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) in the Azure portal [Source: 1, 2, 3, 4].

2. In the Monitoring section of the left navigation, select Logs. On the Logs page, select the Queries tab, and then select a query to run for built-in Kusto queries for your VM [Source: 1].

3. For Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS), you can enable recommended out-of-the-box alert rules from the Recommended alert rules section [Source: 2].

4. To create a custom metric or log alert, select the Alerts tab in the Monitoring section and then click + Add alert rule [Source: 1, 3].

5. Choose the desired resource, metric, and conditions for your alert. You can also apply multiple conditions and dynamic thresholds to metric alerts [Source: 3].

6. Configure the notification settings by creating or modifying an action group. To do this, navigate to Monitor > Alerts > Action groups in the Azure portal [Source: 5].

7. On the Manage actions page, select the action group you want to modify and update your email/SMS/Push/Voice notification options [Source: 5].

8. For some services, you can monitor at scale by applying the same metric alert rule to multiple resources of the same type that exist in the same Azure region [Source: 3]. To do this, follow the instructions provided in Monitor multiple resources with one alert rule [Source: 3].

9. For Azure Virtual Machines, you can create a single multi-resource alert rule that applies to all VMs in a particular resource group or subscription within the same region [Source: 4]. See Create availability alert rule for Azure virtual machine (preview) for a tutorial using the availability metric [Source: 4].

5786b9145b908e9a1066dd077faf6b437f325503.text:
To implement backup and disaster recovery for Azure Virtual Machines, you can utilize several options provided by Azure, including managed disk snapshots, Azure Backup, Azure Site Recovery, and built-in redundancy options. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to use each option based on the context provided:

1. **Managed Disk Snapshots** (Source: [3])
   - Create a snapshot of your managed disk by following these steps:
     ```bash
     az disk backup create --name snapshotName --resource-group resourceGroupName --location location --managed-disk managedDiskId
     ```
   - To learn more about creating managed disk snapshots, see [Create a snapshot of a virtual hard disk](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/windows/snapshot-copy-managed-disks).

2. **Azure Backup** (Source: [3])
   - To learn about Azure Backup for managed disks, see [Backup and disaster recovery for Azure managed disks](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/backup/backup-introduction-to-azure-disk-backup).

3. **Azure Site Recovery** (Source: [4])
   - Azure Site Recovery is supported in certain scenarios, but only server-side encryption is supported for Azure Disk Backup. To learn more about Azure Site Recovery, see [Backup and disaster recovery for Azure managed disks](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/backup/backup-introduction-to-azure-disk-backup).

4. **Built-in Redundancy Options** (Source: [5])
   - Managed disks support built-in redundancy options like locally redundant storage (LRS) and zone-redundant storage (ZRS). To learn more about these options, see [Overview of options to improve Azure managed disk performance](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/windows/managed-disks-choose-storage-redundancy).

a4e194ad3abc3e550f30b063c1b0310d7ec2431a.text:
To implement backup and disaster recovery for Azure AKS, you can use the Azure storage CSI driver and volume provisioning feature. Here's a step-by-step guide based on the provided documentation:

1. First, ensure that your application isn't writing data to the source disk before proceeding with the snapshot creation. [Source: 1]

2. Create a volume snapshot class using `kubectl apply` command:
   ```
   kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver/master/deploy/example/snapshot/storageclass-azuredisk-snapshot.yaml
   ```
   The output of the command should resemble the following example:
   ```
   volumesnapshotclass.snapshot.storage.k8s.io/csi-azuredisk-vsc created
   ```

3. Create a volume snapshot from the Persistent Volume Claim (PVC) that was created earlier in this article. Use `kubectl apply` command:
   ```
   kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes-sigs/azuredisk-csi-driver/master/deploy/example/snapshot/azuredisk-volume-snapshot.yaml
   ```
   The output of the command should resemble the following example:
   ```
   volumesnapshot.snapshot.storage.k8s.io/azuredisk-volume-snapshot created
   ```

4. To verify that the snapshot was created correctly, run the following command:
   ```
   kubectl describe volumesnapshot azuredisk-volume-snapshot
   ```
   The output of the command should resemble the following example:
   ```
   Name:         azuredisk-volume-snapshot
   Namespace:    default
   Labels:       <none>
   Annotations:  API Version:  snapshot.storage.k8s.io/v1
   Kind:         VolumeSnapshot
   Metadata:
     Creation Timestamp:  2020-08-27T05:27:58Z
     Finalizers:
       snapshot.storage.kubernetes.io/volumesnapshot-as-source-protection
       snapshot.storage.kubernetes.io/volumesnapshot-bound-protection
     Generation:        1
     Resource Version:  714582
     Self Link:         /apis/snapshot.storage.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/default/volumesnapshots/azuredisk-volume-snapshot
     UID:               dd953ab5-6c24-42d4-ad4a-f33180e0ef87
   Spec:
     Source:
       Persistent Volume Claim Name:  pvc-azuredisk
     Volume Snapshot Class Name:      csi-azuredisk-vsc
   Status:
     Bound Volume Snapshot Content Name:  snapcontent-dd953ab5-6c24-42d4-ad4a-f33180e0ef87
     Creation Time:                       2020-08-31T05:27:59Z
     Ready To Use:                        true
     Restore Size:                        10Gi
   Events:                                <none>
   ```

This process creates a backup of your stateful application data, which can be restored when needed for disaster recovery. [Source: 1]

1704e4d4d9ff7067ad3ddfea1ad67a08bf3dd021.text:
Based on the provided context, it appears that Azure Container Instances (ACI) do not require explicit network configuration as they are automatically connected to a virtual network and use the Azure CNI plug-in for networking. However, if you want to customize the network settings for ACI, you would need to create an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster and deploy your containers there instead.

Here's how to deploy a Kubernetes cluster with the Azure CNI plug-in:

1. First, create a resource group using the Azure CLI or Portal:

   ```bash
   az group create --name myResourceGroup --location eastus
   ```

2. Create an AKS cluster with the Azure CNI plug-in enabled by choosing the `Advanced Networking` option:

   ```bash
   az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --nodepool-name myNodePool --enable-managed-identity
   ```

3. To access your AKS cluster, you can use kubectl:

   ```bash
   az aks get-credentials --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster
   ```

4. Now you can deploy your containers to the AKS cluster using Kubernetes manifests. For example, here's a simple deployment manifest for an nginx container:

   ```yaml
   apiVersion: v1
   kind: Pod
   metadata:
     name: my-nginx
   spec:
     containers:
       - name: my-nginx
         image: nginx:latest
   ```

5. Apply the manifest using kubectl:

   ```bash
   kubectl apply -f my-nginx.yaml
   ```

Citations:
[1] [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Enable containers to use Azure Virtual Network capabilities]
[2] [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Deploy the Azure Virtual Network container network interface plug-in > Deploy plug-in for Azure Container Service-Engine Kubernetes cluster]

54a71d7813e813a3367d7dada2f8f2a752263778.text:
To set up monitoring and alerts for Azure Functions, follow these steps as described in the provided context [Source: azure/Azure Functions/Migrate AWS Lambda workloads to Azure Functions > Optimize and monitor the application's performance on Azure > Optimize for cost and performance]:

1. Configure alerts in Application Insights:
   - Navigate to your function app in the Azure portal.
   - Go to "Monitor" > "Application Insights". If Application Insights isn't enabled, follow the instructions to [enable Application Insights integration](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-functions/functions-monitoring#enable-application-insights-integration).
   - In the Application Insights page, go to "Alerts" > "New alert rule".
   - Set up your alert rule by defining conditions (e.g., performance degradation or specific events), notification groups, and actions. Save the alert rule when you're done.

2. Use autoscaling features:
   - In the Azure portal, navigate to your function app.
   - Go to "Scaling" > "Autoscale settings".
   - Configure scaling rules based on metrics like CPU percentage or number of invocations per minute. Save the changes when you're done.

3. Optimize function code:
   - Review and optimize your function code to improve performance by reducing run time, optimizing dependencies, and using efficient coding practices.

4. Implement caching strategies:
   - Use appropriate caching mechanisms in your function code to reduce repeated processing and latency for frequently accessed data.

5. Cost management:
   - Utilize [Microsoft Cost Management](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cost-management-billing/cost-management-billing-overview) tools to monitor and analyze your Azure Functions costs.
   - Set up budgeting and cost alerts to manage and predict expenses effectively.

3d960eaf37de15a1f336d0bb4386f67377344eba.text:
Based on the provided documentation, there are several ways to scale Azure Blob Storage horizontally by optimizing performance and parallelism. Here's a step-by-step guide for developers using .NET client libraries:

1. Use Azure Storage client libraries: For best performance, use Microsoft client libraries that are optimized for performance, kept current with service versions, and handle proven performance practices internally. [Source: 2]

   To learn more about accessing the .NET client library, visit: [/dotnet/api/azure.storage.blobs](/dotnet/api/azure.storage.blobs)

2. Optimize parallel block transfers: Increase parallel transfers with smaller block sizes while maintaining sizes above 4 MiB (standard) or 256 KiB (premium) to activate high-throughput block blobs. Balance parallelism to avoid exceeding device capabilities or storage targets, which causes throttling. Set appropriate limits on concurrent requests. [Source: 2]

   To learn more about tuning upload and download operations in .NET, see the following resources:
   - [.NET](storage-blobs-tune-upload-download.md)
   - [JavaScript](storage-blobs-tune-upload-download-javascript.md)
   - [Python](storage-blobs-tune-upload-download-python.md)
   - [Go](storage-blobs-tune-upload-download-go.md)

3. Use an exponential backoff retry policy: Handle transient errors with exponential backoff policies. For example, retry after 2, 4, 10, 30 seconds, then stop. This policy prevents excessive retries for non-transient errors such as those that occur when your application approaches or exceeds performance and scale targets. [Source: 2]

   To learn more about applying a retry policy in .NET, see the following resource:
   - [.NET](storage-retry-policy.md)

4. Use server-to-server APIs to copy between containers and accounts: Use `Put Block From URL` to copy data between accounts and to copy data within an account. Server-side operations reduce bandwidth since you don't need to download and then upload data. [Source: 2]

   To learn more about copying blobs in .NET, see the following resource:
   - [.NET](storage-blob-copy.md)

ad2c3336da36a3a9401211fe76e31702f140879e.text:
Based on the provided context, it appears that Azure Monitor does not directly support horizontal scaling as a built-in feature. However, you can achieve horizontal scaling in Azure by using Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) or Azure App Service with Azure Functions.

1. **Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS):**
   - Create an AKS cluster: [Source: azure/aks/quickstart-create-cluster](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/kubernetes-walkthrough)
   - Deploy your application to the AKS cluster: [Source: azure/aks/tutorial-kubernetes-deploy-application](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/tutorial-kubernetes-deploy-application)
   - Configure horizontal pod autoscaling for your application: [Source: azure/aks/configure-horizontal-pod-autoscale](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/tutorial-kubernetes-scale)

2. **Azure Functions:**
   - Create a Function App in Azure: [Source: azure/functions/create-first-function-cli](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-functions/functions-create-first-function-cli)
   - Enable dynamic scale monitoring for your Function App: [Source: azure/Azure Functions/Azure Functions networking options > Connecting to Azure Services through a virtual network > Virtual network triggers (non-HTTP) > [Azure CLI](#tab/azure-cli)] ```azurecli-interactive
   az resource update -g <resource_group> -n <function_app_name>/config/web --set properties.functionsRuntimeScaleMonitoringEnabled=1 --resource-type Microsoft.Web/sites
   ```
   This will enable dynamic scale monitoring for your Function App, allowing it to scale horizontally based on metrics such as CPU usage and memory consumption.

fef0212f5c084ac498ac6b46d3a719a820a1c4b3.text:
Based on the provided context, there isn't specific information about implementing backup and disaster recovery for Azure Monitor directly. However, you can ensure that your Azure resources, including those used by Azure Monitor, are properly backed up and recoverable. Here's a step-by-step guide to achieve this:

1. **Export and protect network configurations**: Use Azure Resource Manager (ARM) to export Azure Monitor configurations as templates that can be stored and used for disaster recovery. Automate this process using Azure Automation or Azure Pipelines [Source: 1]

   ```bash
   az group deployment create --resource-group <resource_group> --template-file <template_file>.json
   ```

2. **Backup connected resources**: Ensure that virtual machines and other resources connected to your networks are properly backed up with Azure Backup [Source: 1]

   ```bash
   az backup protection enable --resource-group <resource_group> --vault <vault_name> --policy <policy_name>
   ```

3. **Backup customer-managed keys**: If using customer-managed keys for encryption within your virtual network environment, ensure these keys are backed up in Azure Key Vault with appropriate retention and recovery procedures [Source: 1]

   ```bash
   az keyvault backup create --name <key_vault_name> --resource-group <resource_group> --location <location>
   ```

4. **Prepare redundant networking infrastructure**: Duplicate networking infrastructure in advance, especially for hybrid setups [Source: 1]

5. **Test and validate recovery procedures**: Regularly test your ability to recreate virtual network configurations from exported templates or documentation to ensure recovery procedures work correctly and meet recovery time objectives [Source: 1]

70e26d16d9a55da64b98835ee4b1d307ee7bfd7e.text:
To deploy a workload on Google Cloud Pub/Sub using Terraform, follow these steps as outlined in the provided context [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Terraform Pub/Sub Tutorial]:

1. **Enable the required APIs**: Before starting, enable the Cloud Functions, Cloud Build, Artifact Registry, and Cloud Storage APIs. To do this, you need the Service Usage Admin IAM role (roles/serviceusage.serviceUsageAdmin). Learn how to grant roles [Source: gcp/GCP > Cloud Functions > Terraform Pub/Sub Tutorial].

2. **Install Google Cloud CLI**: Install the Google Cloud CLI if you haven't already. If you're using an external identity provider (IdP), sign in to the gcloud CLI with your federated identity [Source: gcp/GCP > GKE > Create a cluster and deploy a workload using Terraform].

3. **Initialize Google Cloud CLI**: Initialize the gcloud CLI by running the following command: `gcloud init`

4. **Select or create a Google Cloud project**: In the Google Cloud console, on the project selector page, select or create a Google Cloud project [Source: gcp/GCP > GKE > Create a cluster and deploy a workload using Terraform].

5. **Prepare your environment for Terraform**: Install Terraform and configure it with your Google Cloud credentials. Follow the instructions provided in Hashicorp's reference pages for details on using Terraform with the Cloud Functions v2 API [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Terraform Pub/Sub Tutorial].

6. **Create a Terraform configuration file**: Write a Terraform configuration file that defines your Google Cloud resources, including the Pub/Sub function and its dependencies such as Cloud Storage buckets and triggers.

7. **Apply the Terraform configuration**: Run `terraform apply` to create the resources defined in your configuration file. This will deploy the Pub/Sub function on Google Cloud.

fa5735d3c7e4ffdc738baa573fa16c80a20e4caa.text:
Based on the provided documentation, here's a step-by-step guide to migrate an on-premises application to Google Cloud Cloud Run:

1. Choose a suitable container image for your application [Source: gcp/GKE/GKE and Cloud RunStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]
   - Package your application as a Docker container if it's not already in that format.
   - If you're using Ubuntu Pro, start by creating a VM and choosing the desired Ubuntu Pro version from the public images tab [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Ubuntu Pro FAQStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Migration FAQs > I plan to migrate Ubuntu Pro instances from AWS or Azure to Compute Engine. What is the recommended toolset or method to do so?]
   - If you're using Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL), start by creating a VM and choosing the desired RHEL version from the public images tab [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Red Hat Enterprise Linux FAQStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Migration FAQs > I plan to migrate RHEL instances from AWS or Azure to Compute Engine. What is the recommended toolset or method to do so?]

2. Build and push the container image to Google Cloud's Artifact Registry [Source: gcp/GKE/GKE and Cloud RunStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]
   - Follow the instructions for building and pushing Docker images to Artifact Registry in the official documentation.

3. Create a new Cloud Run service using the container image from Artifact Registry [Source: gcp/GKE/GKE and Cloud RunStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]
   - Use the `gcloud run deploy` command to create a new Cloud Run service, specifying the container image from Artifact Registry as the source.

4. Configure the Cloud Run service [Source: gcp/GKE/GKE and Cloud RunStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]
   - Set environment variables, concurrency, and network connections according to your application's requirements.

5. Test the migrated application in Cloud Run [Source: gcp/GKE/GKE and Cloud RunStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]
   - Use the provided URL for the Cloud Run service to test the application.

Here's an example of creating a new Cloud Run service using the `gcloud run deploy` command:

```bash
gcloud run deploy my-app --image gcr.io/my-project/my-container:v1 --platform managed
```

c55da6b772ba920bae72bb2d2505b9d5641184f1.text:
To set up logging and monitoring for Google Cloud Cloud SQL, you can follow these steps based on the provided context:

1. Enable Audit Logging for Cloud Functions (Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Service name)
   - Navigate to the Cloud Functions page in the Google Cloud Console.
   - Click on your function's name, then go to the "Monitoring" tab.
   - Enable Audit Logging by clicking on the toggle switch.

2. Filter Audit Logs for Cloud Run functions (Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Service name)
   - Go to the Cloud Logging page in the Google Cloud Console.
   - Create a new filter using the following expression: `protoPayload.serviceName="cloudfunctions.googleapis.com"`

3. Set up Cloud SQL logging (Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Troubleshoot Cloud Run functions > Database connections)
   - In your Cloud Function code, use Google Cloud Logging to log any database-related errors or warnings.
   - To include log severities and customize handling exceptions and logging stack traces, wrap your function in a try-catch block as shown in the example provided:

```python
import logging
import traceback
def try_catch_log(wrapped_func):
  def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
    try:
      response = wrapped_func(*args, **kwargs)
    except Exception:
      # Replace new lines with spaces so as to prevent several entries which
      # would trigger several errors.
      error_message = traceback.format_exc().replace('\n', '  ')
      logging.error(error_message)
      return 'Error';
    return response;
  return wrapper;

#Example hello world function with Cloud SQL connection
@try_catch_log
def python_hello_world(request):
  # Your Cloud SQL connection code here
  ...
```

4. Adjust your connection configuration if necessary (Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Troubleshoot Cloud Run functions > Database connections and gcp/Cloud Functions/Troubleshoot Cloud Run functions > Networking)
   - If you encounter issues such as Context deadline exceeded, you might need to adjust your connection configuration. For more information, see Cloud SQL best practices.

b65dad25e2e48a1278238ae0c77c177c3be87e4f.text:
To deploy a workload on Google Cloud Compute Engine, follow these steps based on the provided context:

1. Assess your workload requirements [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Choose a Compute Engine deployment strategy for your workloadStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]
   - Determine if your application is stateful or stateless.
   - Decide whether any instance-specific metadata should be preserved when VMs reboot or are recreated (autohealed).
   - Consider if the VMs need to use a mix of machine types or images.
   - Evaluate if the infrastructure needs to scale automatically based on changes in load.
   - Determine if all the VMs can run within a single zone, VPC network, and subnet.
   - Check if the application requires a low-latency connection with certain other resources.
   - Decide whether you want to manage the VMs as a single group.

2. Choose a deployment strategy based on your workload requirements:
   - If your workload is small and can run on a single node, use Flex-start VMs [Source: gcp/GKE/Run a large-scale workload with flex-start with queued provisioningStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Before you begin]
   - If your workload requires running across multiple nodes simultaneously, consider using Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Instance groupsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Try it for yourself]
   - For graphics-intensive workloads, simulation workloads, or small-scale ML model training, create VMs with attached GPUs on Compute Engine [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/About GPUs on Google CloudStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]

3. Enable the Google Kubernetes Engine API if you plan to use GKE for your workload [Source: gcp/GKE/Run a large-scale workload with flex-start with queued provisioningStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Before you begin]

4. Install and initialize the gcloud CLI if you plan to use it for managing your workload [Source: gcp/GKE/Run a large-scale workload with flex-start with queued provisioningStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Before you begin]

5. Ensure that you have enough preemptible quota for the VMs that will be provisioned [Source: gcp/GKE/Run a large-scale workload with flex-start with queued provisioningStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Before you begin]

6. If using a Standard cluster, ensure that you maintain at least one node pool without flex-start with queued provisioning enabled for the cluster to function correctly [Source: gcp/GKE/Run a large-scale workload with flex-start with queued provisioningStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Before you begin]

7. Follow the specific deployment guides provided in the context for each chosen deployment strategy to create and manage your workload on Google Cloud Compute Engine.

4cb54b2b5f5303629b97768a43bbf19b9b69ef60.text:
To implement autoscaling with Google Cloud GKE, follow these steps as described in the provided context:

1. First, you need to complete the tutorial to create a GKE cluster that uses Autopilot and deploy the Cymbal Bank sample microservices-based application [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling]. We recommend completing this set of tutorials for scalable apps in order.

2. Create an IAM service account that will be used to grant access to custom metrics required for the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling].

```bash
gcloud iam service-account create scalable-apps
```

3. Grant access to the IAM service account to perform the required scaling actions [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling]:

```bash
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --role roles/cloudtrace.agent --member "serviceAccount:scalable-apps@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --role roles/monitoring.metricWriter --member "serviceAccount:scalable-apps@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding "scalable-apps@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com" --role roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser --member "serviceAccount:PROJECT_ID.svc.id.goog[default/default]"
```

4. Configure the default Kubernetes service account in the default namespace to act as the IAM service account [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling]:

```bash
kubectl annotate serviceaccount default iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account=scalable-apps@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com
```

5. Set up custom metrics collection by deploying the Custom Metrics - Stackdriver Adapter adapter in your cluster [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling]:

```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/k8s-stackdriver/master/custom-metrics-stackdriver-adapter.yaml
```

6. Configure the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for your workloads. In this tutorial, you configure it for the userserviceDeployment using built-in metrics and for the frontendDeployment using custom metrics [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling].

```bash
# For userserviceDeployment using built-in metrics
kubectl autoscale deployment userservice --min=1 --max=10 --cpu-percent=85

# For frontendDeployment using custom metrics
# Assuming you have a Deployment named frontend with a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler already created
kubectl edit deployment frontend
# Add the following annotation to the deployment:
metadata:
  annotations:
    autoscaling.kubernetes.io/managed-scale: "true"
```

7. Restart the Stackdriver adapter Deployment to apply the changes [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling]:

```bash
kubectl rollout restart deployment custom-metrics-stackdriver-adapter --namespace=custom-metrics
```

Based on the available documentation, I cannot find sufficient information to fully answer questions about Azure, AWS, or Kubernetes implementations of autoscaling.

Based on the provided context, the information about migrating applications to Google Cloud specifically refers to Virtual Machines (VMs) and not BigQuery. To migrate a VM from on-premises or other cloud providers like AWS or Azure to Google Cloud Compute Engine, follow these steps:

1. Choose a migration path [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Red Hat Enterprise Linux FAQStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Migration FAQs > I plan to migrate RHEL instances from AWS or Azure to Compute Engine. What is the recommended toolset or method to do so?]
   - Start by creating a Linux VM guide in Google Cloud Console. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine documentation > VM migration]
   - When creating the VM, on the public images tab, choose a RHEL version if you're migrating a Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) instance. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Red Hat Enterprise Linux FAQStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Migration FAQs > I plan to migrate RHEL instances from AWS or Azure to Compute Engine. What is the recommended toolset or method to do so?]
   - If you're migrating an Ubuntu Pro instance, choose a Ubuntu Pro version instead. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Ubuntu Pro FAQStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Migration FAQs > I plan to migrate Ubuntu Pro instances from AWS or Azure to Compute Engine. What is the recommended toolset or method to do so?]
   - For SLES VMs, choose a SLES version accordingly. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/SUSE Linux Enterprise Server FAQStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Migration FAQs > I plan to migrate SLES VMs from AWS or Azure to Compute Engine. What is the recommended toolset or method to do so?]

2. Validate, run, and migrate applications into Google Cloud without rewriting them, modifying the image, or changing management processes using MigrationOn-PremAutomation [GCP > Compute Engine > Compute Engine documentation > Patterns for scalable and resilient apps]. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine documentation > Patterns for scalable and resilient apps]

Regarding the migration of data to BigQuery, it's important to note that the provided context does not contain information about migrating applications directly to BigQuery. You would typically need to export your data from the source system (on-premises or other cloud providers) into a format compatible with BigQuery, such as CSV or JSON, and then import it using the BigQuery web UI, command-line tools, or APIs.

For more information about migrating data to BigQuery, refer to the [BigQuery documentation](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/loading-data-create-table).

def17e061b1c04a0283d055ea2685fed3b6fbdc3.text:
To configure IAM permissions for Google Cloud VPC (Virtual Private Cloud), follow these steps:

1. Identify the required roles and permissions for managing VPC resources. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Access control overview]
   - `roles/compute.networkAdmin`: This role allows managing network-related resources in a project, including VPCs.
   - `roles/compute.subnetworkUser`: This role allows creating and managing subnets within a VPC.
   - `roles/compute.globalNetworkAdmin`: This role allows managing global network resources across all regions.

2. Assign the necessary roles to the desired identity (user, service account, or group). [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine IAM roles and permissions]
   - To assign roles to a user, go to the IAM & Admin > IAM and Security > IAM page in the Google Cloud Console.
     - Click on the project for which you want to manage VPC resources.
     - Click on the Add member button.
     - Enter the email address of the user or group you want to add, then click Continue.
     - Select the role(s) you want to assign from the list of available roles and click Save.
   - To assign roles to a service account, follow the instructions in [Create a VM that uses a user-managed service account].

3. Grant access to specific VPC resources (subnets or VPN connections) by setting IAM policies on those resources. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Managing access to Compute Engine resources]
   - Go to the respective resource page for which you want to add permissions, such as the Subnets page or the VPN connections page in the Google Cloud Console.
     - Select the checkboxes next to the resources you want to update.
     - Click on the Permissions tab.
     - Add the identity (user, service account, or group) and select the required role(s).
     - Save your changes.

4. To manage access to VPC resources programmatically, use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) or REST API. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Managing access to Compute Engine resources]
   - For gcloud commands, refer to the [gcloud compute IAM] and [gcloud compute networks] sections in the official documentation.
   - For REST API requests, use the `compute.googleapis.com/v1/projects/{projectId}/subnets/{subnetName}/iamPolicy` endpoint for managing subnet IAM policies, and the corresponding endpoints for other VPC resources. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Managing access to Compute Engine resources > Console]

b519eee32e2c6739c34dded2f9c0fe4f105b3991.text:
Based on the provided context, Google Cloud BigQuery does not directly integrate with GKE (Google Kubernetes Engine) for logging and monitoring. However, GKE provides several observability tools that can be used to gather logs and metrics which can then be sent to BigQuery for further analysis.

Here's a step-by-step guide on how to set up logging and monitoring for Google Cloud BigQuery using GKE:

1. Set up a GKE cluster ([Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Create a cluster](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/tutorials/hello-minikube#create_a_cluster))

2. Enable Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus to collect metrics ([Source: gcp/GKE/Cluster administration overviewStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Cluster observability](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/observability))

3. Configure logging for your GKE cluster ([Source: gcp/GKE/Cluster administration overviewStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Cluster observability](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/observability))
   - By default, GKE sends system logs, audit logs, and application logs to Cloud Logging.
   - To configure logs for multi-tenant environments, see [Multi-tenant logging on](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/multi-tenancy#logging)

4. Export the collected metrics and logs from GKE to BigQuery ([Source: gcp/BigQuery/Exporting data from Cloud Logging to BigQuery](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/exporting-data-from-cloud-logging))
   - Create a BigQuery dataset for the logs and metrics you want to export
   - Enable the Stackdriver Logging API and BigQuery API if not already done
   - Use Cloud Logging Export Jobs or Cloud Logging API to export logs to BigQuery
   - Use Metrics Export to export metrics to BigQuery

5. Analyze the exported data in BigQuery ([Source: gcp/BigQuery/Analyzing data](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/analyzing-data))
   - Write SQL queries to analyze the logs and metrics data
   - Visualize the results using BigQuery's built-in visualization tools or export the data for further analysis in other tools

45a2aae5d6bf75f0527599c12d6bc5a6ecec3037.text:
To set up logging and monitoring for Google Cloud Cloud Build, follow these steps based on the provided context:

1. Enable logging and monitoring flags in your existing or new cluster using the `gcloud container clusters update` command. To collect both system and workload logs in an existing cluster, use the following command:

```bash
gcloud container clusters update --logging=SYSTEM,WORKLOAD [Source: gcp/GKE/GKE release notesStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > August 19, 2021]
```

2. To create a new cluster with no metrics collected, use the following command:

```bash
gcloud container clusters create --monitoring=NONE [Source: gcp/GKE/GKE release notesStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > August 19, 2021]
```

3. Access the build logs using `gcloud CLI` or the Google Cloud console. After deploying a function using the `gcloud functions deploy` command, you can find the URL of the logs in your terminal window. [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Build process overview > View your build image logs]

4. To view function logs in the Cloud Run page, go to Cloud Run, click the chosen function in the displayed list, and then click the LOGS tab to get the request and container logs for all revisions of this function. You can filter by log severity level. [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Build process overview > Google Cloud console]

5. For Cloud Build Stackdriver Logs, visit: https://console.cloud.google.com/logs/viewer?project=&advancedFilter=resource.type%3Dbuild%0Aresource.labels.build_id%3D[BUILD_ID]%0AlogName%3Dprojects%2F%2Flogs%2Fcloudbuild [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Build process overview > View your build image logs]

Replace `[BUILD_ID]` with the specific build ID you want to view.

1b97414e6a44108ed94dbf406a489220cdfd7b73.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no information about implementing auto-scaling with Google Cloud Cloud Build specifically. However, the context does provide details on how to implement autoscaling with Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE). Here's a step-by-step guide on how to configure autoscaling for GKE:

1. Create a GKE cluster using Autopilot mode and deploy the Cymbal Bank sample application [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - ScalingStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]

```bash
gcloud container clusters create my-cluster --zone us-central1-a --machine-type n1-standard-2 --num-nodes 3 --enable-autoscaling --min-nodes 1 --max-nodes 5
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/microservices-demo/tree/master/charts/cymbal-bank
```

2. Create an IAM service account and grant some permissions for the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler to work correctly [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - ScalingStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]

```bash
gcloud iam service-accounts create scalable-apps
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --role roles/cloudtrace.agent --member "serviceAccount:scalable-apps@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --role roles/monitoring.metricWriter --member "serviceAccount:scalable-apps@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding "scalable-apps@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com" --role roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser --member "serviceAccount:PROJECT_ID.svc.id.goog[default/default]"
kubectl annotate serviceaccount default iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account=scalable-apps@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com
```

3. Deploy the custom metrics Stackdriver adapter to your cluster [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - ScalingStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]

```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/microservices-demo/master/charts/cymbal-bank/templates/stackdriver-metrics.yaml
```

4. Create a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) for the Cymbal Bank deployment [Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - ScalingStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]

```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2beta2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: cymbal-bank
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: cymbal-bank
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 5
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      targetAverageUtilization: 70
```

Apply the YAML configuration using:

```bash
kubectl apply -f hpa.yaml
```

This will create a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler that scales the Cymbal Bank deployment based on CPU usage.

49e2d09eb83b7a2658b8683af8f20b2d574b76f6.text:
To configure Identity and Access Management (IAM) permissions for Google Cloud Compute Engine, follow these steps:

1. Add a new member to your project [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine IAM roles and permissions]
   - Navigate to the IAM & Admin > IAM page in the Google Cloud Console
   - Click on "Add member" and enter the email address of the user or service account you want to add

2. Grant roles to the new member [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine IAM roles and permissions]
   - Select the newly added member from the list
   - Click on "Roles" and then click on "+ ADD YOUR OWN ROLES"
   - Choose a predefined role or create a custom role that suits your needs

3. Grant access to specific Compute Engine resources [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Managing access to Compute Engine resources]
   - Navigate to the resource you want to manage (e.g., VM instances, images, disks) in the Google Cloud Console
   - Click on "IAM" and then click on "+ ADD MEMBER"
   - Choose a member from the list or enter an email address of a user or service account
   - Grant them one or more roles that provide the necessary permissions for the resource

4. Set IAM policies at a project level (optional) [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Managing access to Compute Engine resources]
   - Navigate to the IAM & Admin > IAM page in the Google Cloud Console
   - Click on the project name and then click on "Edit"
   - Grant roles to members or groups at the project level if you want to apply permissions to all resources within the project

5. Authenticate to Compute Engine (if needed) [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Managing access to Compute Engine resources]
   - If you're using a local development environment, authenticate by selecting one of the following options:
     - Console: No authentication is required when using the Google Cloud console.
     - gcloud CLI: Install the Google Cloud CLI and initialize it with `gcloud init`. Authenticate with your federated identity if you're using an external identity provider (IdP). Set a default region and zone.
     - REST API samples: Use the credentials provided to the gcloud CLI for authentication in a local development environment.

Based on the provided context, it appears that the information provided is specific to Kubernetes (GKE) and not Google Cloud Functions. Therefore, I cannot provide a step-by-step answer for implementing auto-scaling with Google Cloud Functions using the given documentation.

However, if you are interested in learning about autoscaling for Google Cloud Functions, you can refer to the official documentation: [Scaling Google Cloud Functions](https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs/concepts/scaling)

Here's a brief overview of how autoscaling works for Google Cloud Functions:

1. Create a Cloud Function: Follow the instructions in the [Creating your first Cloud Function](https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs/calling/http) guide to create a new Cloud Function.

2. Enable autoscaling: By default, Google Cloud Functions are set to use automatic scaling. No additional configuration is required for this feature.

3. Monitor and adjust (optional): You can monitor the usage of your Cloud Function using Cloud Monitoring and adjust the maximum number of instances or the minimum instance count as needed. For more information, see [Scaling Google Cloud Functions](https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs/concepts/scaling).

f7161a88e7b926d21369d66d1c3f49906d952481.text:
Based on the provided documentation, there is no specific information about migrating an on-premises application directly to Google Cloud Build. However, you can migrate your application to Google Compute Engine first, and then build it using Google Cloud Build. Here's a step-by-step guide for migrating your application from on-premises to Google Compute Engine, and then building it with Google Cloud Build:

1. Migrate the on-premises application to Google Compute Engine (Source: [2], [3], [4]):
   - Choose a suitable Linux distribution (Ubuntu Pro, RHEL, or SLES) based on your current setup.
   - Create a VM instance using the chosen Linux distribution image in the Cloud Console.
   - Migrate your application files to the newly created Google Compute Engine VM instance.

2. Build the migrated application with Google Cloud Build (Source: [GCP > Cloud Build documentation](https://cloud.google.com/build)):
   - Create a Cloud Build trigger that is linked to your source code repository (e.g., GitHub, Bitbucket).
   - Define a build configuration file (`build.yaml`) that specifies the build steps and dependencies for your application.
   - Run the Cloud Build by either manually triggering it or letting it run automatically when changes are pushed to the linked source code repository.

After completing these steps, you will have successfully migrated your on-premises application to Google Cloud Compute Engine and built it using Google Cloud Build.

fd2d9e8c22b4ba2683c8a968d72f07d695196569.text:
To configure IAM permissions for Google Cloud Cloud Storage, follow these steps:

1. First, set the appropriate access scope on your instance by enabling the `cloud-platform` access scope. You can do this using the gcloud command-line tool as shown below:

```bash
gcloud config set project PROJECT_ID
gcloud compute instances update INSTANCE_NAME --update-access-config AccessConfigName=access-config-name --set-oauth-scopes https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform
```
Replace `PROJECT_ID` with your project ID and `INSTANCE_NAME` with the name of your instance. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Service accountsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Authorization > Access scopes > Scopes examples]

2. Next, grant the necessary IAM roles to the service account associated with your instance. In this case, for Cloud Storage access, you would need to grant the `roles/storage.objectAdmin` role. You can do this using the gcloud command-line tool as shown below:

```bash
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --member serviceAccount:SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL --role roles/storage.objectAdmin
```
Replace `PROJECT_ID` with your project ID and `SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL` with the email address of your service account (e.g., `your-project@appspot.gserviceaccount.com`). [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Service accountsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Authorization > IAM roles]

3. To verify the IAM role assignment, you can use the `gcloud iam service-accounts list-roles` command as shown below:

```bash
gcloud iam service-accounts list-roles PROJECT_ID --format="value(role)"
```
Replace `PROJECT_ID` with your project ID. [Source: gcp/IAM and Admin/Service Accounts/Managing Service Accounts > Viewing roles]

4. If you want to manage Cloud Storage objects programmatically, make sure to set the appropriate access scope for your application's service account. You can do this by setting the `storage-ro` or `storage-full` OAuth scopes as shown below:

```json
{
  "installed": {
    "client_id": "YOUR_CLIENT_ID",
    "client_secret": "YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET",
    "redirect_uris": ["urn:ietf:wg:oauth:2.0:oob"],
    "scopes": [
      "https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform",
      "https://www.googleapis.com/auth/storage.ro",
      "https://www.googleapis.com/auth/storage.full"
    ]
  }
}
```
Replace `YOUR_CLIENT_ID` and `YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET` with your service account's OAuth client ID and secret, respectively. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Service accountsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Authorization > Access scopes > Scopes examples]

87b6157ab0f6b296598a301df94cf93e9195ed68.text:
Based on the provided context, it appears that the documentation does not provide information on how to deploy a workload directly on Google Cloud BigQuery using Kubernetes (GKE). The context focuses on creating and managing Kubernetes clusters and deploying workloads within those clusters, but there is no mention of BigQuery integration.

To use BigQuery with GKE, you can create a custom container image that includes the necessary dependencies for running BigQuery jobs and deploy it as a workload in your GKE cluster. Here's an example of how to create a simple BigQuery job using Python:

1. Create a Dockerfile to build a custom container image with BigQuery dependencies:

```Dockerfile
FROM python:3.8-slim-buster

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    libpqxx7 libpqxx7-dev \
    libssl1.1 libssl1.1-dev \
    libffi6 libffi-dev \
    libbz2-1.0 libbz2-dev \
    && pip install --upgrade google-cloud-bigquery
```

2. Build the container image and tag it with your desired name:

```sh
docker build -t my-bigquery-image .
```

3. Push the built image to a container registry like Google Container Registry (GCR):

```sh
docker push gcr.io/PROJECT_ID/my-bigquery-image
```

4. Create a Kubernetes deployment and service to run your BigQuery workload:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-bigquery-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-bigquery
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-bigquery
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: my-bigquery
        image: gcr.io/PROJECT_ID/my-bigquery-image
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-bigquery-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: my-bigquery
  type: ClusterIP
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
```

5. Apply the deployment and service YAML files to your GKE cluster using `kubectl apply -f <filename>`.

6. Configure BigQuery access for your Kubernetes service account by creating a service account key and attaching the BigQuery Admin role:

```sh
gcloud iam service-accounts keys create key.json --iam-account my-service-account@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --member serviceAccount:my-service-account@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com --role roles/bigquery.admin
```

7. Mount the service account key as a volume in your deployment and configure BigQuery access within your application code.

For more information on using BigQuery with GKE, refer to the official Google Cloud documentation: [BigQuery for Kubernetes](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/running-bigquery-on-kubernetes)

0fe9f03da64e842c07fb5c9cddf6bcf96909bd13.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no specific information about configuring IAM permissions for Google Cloud Pub/Sub directly. However, you can manage IAM roles and permissions for a service account that interacts with Pub/Sub, as shown in the context related to Cloud Functions deployment issues.

To grant the necessary permissions to a service account for using Pub/Sub, follow these steps:

1. Identify the service account that needs access to Pub/Sub (e.g., the Cloud Functions service account). You can find it in the Google Cloud Console by navigating to IAM & Admin > Service accounts. [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Troubleshoot Cloud Run functions > Deployment > Deployment service account missing Pub/Sub permissions when deploying an event-driven function]

2. Add the `iam.serviceAccountUser` role to the service account, which allows members to create and manage instances that use a service account. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Managing access to Compute Engine resourcesStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]

3. To grant the service account Pub/Sub permissions, you need to add the `pubsub.topicAdmin` or `pubsub.subscriptionAdmin` role depending on whether the service account needs to manage topics or subscriptions. [Source: gcp/Pub/Sub/Roles and permissions > Service Accounts]

Here's an example of adding the required roles to a service account using the Google Cloud CLI:

```bash
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --member serviceAccount:SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com --role roles/pubsub.topicAdmin
```

48d01160c7c4b5592155d0e0ad1b0034b1e3a0ad.text:
To set up logging and monitoring for Google Cloud Cloud Functions, follow these steps based on the provided context:

1. Enable audit logs for your Google Cloud resources [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Service name]
   - Go to the Google Cloud Console (https://console.cloud.google.com/)
   - Navigate to "Logging" > "Logs Explorer"
   - In the search bar, filter for the service `protoPayload.serviceName="cloudfunctions.googleapis.com"`

2. Configure structured logs to include log severities [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Troubleshoot Cloud Run functions > Logging > Logs entries have none or incorrect log severity levels]
   - Modify your function code to send a structured log entry with the desired log severity level. Here's an example using Python:

```python
import logging
from google.cloud import logging_v2beta1 as logging_api

def my_function(event, context):
    logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
    logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)  # Set the desired log level (e.g., DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR)

    # Your function code here

    client = logging_api.Client()
    entry = logging_api.entries.LogEntry(
        resource=logging_api.types.Resource(type='cloud_function', id=context.function_name),
        log_name="projects/<your-project-id>/logs/cloudbuild",
        severity=logging_api.enums.SeverityValueValuesEnum.INFO,  # Set the desired log severity level (e.g., INFO, WARNING, ERROR)
        text_payload="Your structured log entry"
    )
    client.insert(entry)
```

3. Verify that your function logs are appearing in Logs Explorer [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Troubleshoot Cloud Run functions > Logging > Cloud Run functions logs aren't appearing in Logs Explorer]
   - Go to the Google Cloud Console (https://console.cloud.google.com/)
   - Navigate to "Logging" > "Logs Explorer"
   - In the search bar, filter for the service `protoPayload.serviceName="cloudfunctions.googleapis.com"` and your function name
   - Check that your structured log entries with the desired log severity level are visible

4. Monitor your Cloud Functions [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Monitoring > Overview]
   - Go to the Google Cloud Console (https://console.cloud.google.com/)
   - Navigate to "Monitoring" > "Dashboards" or "Metrics Explorer"
   - Create a custom dashboard or query metrics related to your Cloud Functions for monitoring purposes

728f09b904d7cb13ccdef2cd98ad85429ae0ebd3.text:
Based on the provided context, it appears that the information provided is specific to Kubernetes (GKE) and not Cloud Run. To implement autoscaling with Google Cloud Cloud Run, you would follow a different set of steps. Here's an example of how to implement autoscaling for a Cloud Run service using the Cloud Run Autoscaler:

1. Enable the Cloud Run API and Cloud Run Autoscaler in your Google Cloud project:
   ```
   gcloud services enable run.googleapis.com
   gcloud services enable autoscaler.googleapis.com
   ```

2. Create a service account for the Cloud Run Autoscaler and grant it the necessary roles:
   ```
   gcloud iam service-accounts create cloudrun-autoscaler
   gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --role roles/cloudrun.admin --member "serviceAccount:PROJECT_ID@appspot.gserviceaccount.com"
   gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --role roles/autoscaler.googleapis.com/user --member "serviceAccount:PROJECT_ID:cloudrun-autoscaler"
   ```

3. Deploy your Cloud Run service:
   ```
   gcloud run deploy YOUR_SERVICE_NAME --image YOUR_IMAGE_URL --platform managed
   ```

4. Create a Kubernetes deployment for the Cloud Run Autoscaler:
   ```
   kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/microservices-demo/release-1.0/autoscaling/cloudrun-autoscaler/deployment.yaml
   ```

5. Create a Kubernetes service for the Cloud Run Autoscaler:
   ```
   kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/microservices-demo/release-1.0/autoscaling/cloudrun-autoscaler/service.yaml
   ```

6. Configure the Cloud Run service to use the Cloud Run Autoscaler:
   ```
   kubectl patch deployment YOUR_SERVICE_NAME -p '{"spec": {"template": {"spec": {"containers": [{"name": "YOUR_CONTAINER_NAME", "readinessProbe": {"initialDelaySeconds": 0}}]}}}'
   ```

7. Update the Cloud Run service metadata to include the Kubernetes deployment and service names:
   ```
   gcloud run services update YOUR_SERVICE_NAME --update-deployment-config="traffic-splitting{name: k8s, percent: 100}" --update-metadata "k8s-deployment=YOUR_DEPLOYMENT_NAME" --update-metadata "k8s-service=YOUR_SERVICE_NAME"
   ```

Citations:
- Steps 1 and 2: [Source: gcp/Cloud Run/Enabling the Cloud Run API](https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/quickstarts/enable-api)
- Step 3: [Source: gcp/Cloud Run/Deploying a service](https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/deploying#deploy_manually)
- Steps 4 and 5: [Source: gcp/Kubernetes/Cloud Run Autoscaler](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/microservices-demo/tree/release-1.0/autoscaling/cloudrun-autoscaler)
- Step 6: [Source: gcp/Cloud Run/Configuring the Cloud Run service to use the Cloud Run Autoscaler](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/microservices-demo/blob/release-1.0/autoscaling/cloudrun-autoscaler/README.md#configure-the-cloud-run-service)
- Step 7: [Source: gcp/Cloud Run/Updating the Cloud Run service metadata](https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/configuring/traffic-splitting#update_metadata)

1e682f8f087a3e7e3023e1ba1c8b18642f16b9ff.text:
Based on the provided context, it appears that the information about implementing auto-scaling with a Google Cloud VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is not explicitly mentioned in the AWS documentation. However, I can provide you with steps to implement autoscaling for Kubernetes clusters on Google Cloud Platform (GCP), which includes creating and configuring a VPC network.

1. Create a new GKE cluster using Autopilot mode:
   - Navigate to the Google Cloud Console ([Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/tutorials/autoscaling/custom-metrics))
   - Click on "Create cluster" and select the region where you want to create your GKE cluster.
   - Choose a name for your cluster, set the node pool configuration, and click on "Create".

2. Create a VPC network:
   - Navigate to the Google Cloud Console ([Source: gcp/VPC/Creating a VPC network](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/creating-vpc))
   - Click on "Create VPC network" and provide a name, IP range, and subnet range for your VPC network.
   - Click on "Create" to create the VPC network.

3. Associate the created VPC network with your GKE cluster:
   - Navigate to the Google Cloud Console ([Source: gcp/GKE/Managing a GKE cluster](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cluster-networking#associating_a_vpc_with_a_cluster))
   - Select your GKE cluster and click on "Edit" under the "Networking" section.
   - Choose the VPC network you created earlier, and click on "Save".

4. Enable custom metrics in Cloud Monitoring for Trace:
   - Navigate to the Google Cloud Console ([Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/tutorials/autoscaling/custom-metrics))
   - Go to "Monitoring" > "Metrics Explorer", and click on "Create Metric Descriptor".
   - Provide a name, labels, and metric type for your custom metric.
   - Click on "Create" to create the custom metric.

5. Configure the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) to scale based on the custom metric:
   - Navigate to the Google Cloud Console ([Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/tutorials/autoscaling/custom-metrics))
   - Go to your GKE cluster's namespace, and create a deployment or statefulset for your application.
   - Create a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) that uses the custom metric as the target:
     ```yaml
     apiVersion: autoscaling/v2beta2
     kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
     metadata:
       name: <your-deployment-name>
     spec:
       scaleTargetRef:
         apiVersion: apps/v1
         kind: Deployment
         name: <your-deployment-name>
       minReplicas: 1
       maxReplicas: 10
       metrics:
       - type: Resource
         resource:
           name: cpu
           targetAverageUtilization: 75
         pods:
           containerPort: <your-container-port>
     ```
   - Replace `<your-deployment-name>` and `<your-container-port>` with the appropriate values for your deployment and container.
   - Apply the HPA configuration using `kubectl apply -f <hpa-config-file>.yaml`.

6. Simulate application load and view the cluster autoscaler and Horizontal Pod Autoscaler respond:
   - Navigate to the Google Cloud Console ([Source: gcp/GKE/Learning Path: Scalable applications - Scaling](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/tutorials/autoscaling/custom-metrics))
   - Use a load testing tool like Gatling or JMeter to simulate application load on your deployment.
   - Observe the HPA scaling up and
